stop asking me why i couldn't make it to NYC frens, this is why: the Korean government imposed an exit ban for all ex-@terra_money employees today https://twitter.com/...

none of us were notified of this at all; when i found out about this, the south korean prosecution told me they usually don't notify people of this because they might destroy evidence and/or leave the country beforehand

@unifiedh @terra_money They subpoenad you and all Terra employees. This has been all over the news. Korean prosecutors and our investigation teams have been trying to contact all of you, but almost everyone is ghosting. What do you expect? Reap what you sow

clarifying upon my previous statement: 1. I am *willing* to cooperate with the investigation 2. but none of us were officially notified of this ban afterwards 3. and the scope of this travel ban includes people who left in 2019/2020, more than two years ago https://twitter.com/...
15 current and former employees of Terraform Labs have been banned from leaving the country https://www.bloomberg.com/...

TFL & ex-TFL employees are now banned from leaving Korea. Some are still uncontactable internationally. If you enrich yourself from a fraudulent scheme, you deserve consequences. Face your day in court fairly. If you are innocent, speak up and testify. No sleep for the guilty. https://twitter.com/...

By the way, the ex-TFL/TFL line is blurry, because 90% of TFL employees scrubbed all their social media (including LinkedIn) of references to Terra (we have archives, but still), and many worked remotely (it is a distributed international team), so this move is very necessary

Some level of economic tension & decoupling between China & US is inevitable in the context of a bipolar competition and the evidence accumulates with each passing day: Elon Musk's Starlink aid to Ukraine triggers scrutiny in China over US military links https://www.ft.com/...
CHINA scrutinises Elon Musk's dual-use technologies: https://www.ft.com/...

China Military Online of the People's Liberation Army, last month attacked SpaceX's links to the US armed forces, including commercial contracts with the military, and slammed Starlink's capacity to “enhance the US military's combat capability”. via @FT https://www.ft.com/...

Elon Musk's foray into Ukraine via SpaceX and Starlink — “considered critical parts of the US space military industrial complex” — have caused “significant alarm in China”, which believes his presence in China comes with surveillance risks. https://www.ft.com/...

An investigation into Chinese government bidding documents details its huge surveillance apparatus, including ~500M cameras and extensive phone tracking tools
China's Surveillance State Is Growing Bigger and More Invasive. These Documents Reveal How.

China is building a new modern marvel. It's not a dam or a high speed rail, it's the most sophisticated domestic surveillance system in the world. The scale of data collection is staggering. No biometric frontier is neglected. This is how it works: https://www.nytimes.com/...

NYT (@muyixiao, @paulmozur, et al.) investigate the @ChinaFile CCGP procurement dataset (100k notices of 17 million+) to map PRC's increasingly integrated #surveillance system of systems (facial, voice, iris, and DNA biometrics; & IMSI 'electric fences'): https://www.nytimes.com/...

interesting investigation into China's surveillance capabilities based on government bidding documents. though it seems right now the gov is obsessed on collecting as much data as possible - dna, voiceprints, etc - without being able to connect/analyze it https://www.nytimes.com/...

China's first internet-age innovation was the Great Firewall that tamed the internet. Many have come since. This project is perhaps most important. The goal is to use technology to gain a more perfect knowledge and control over people. They are succeeding. https://www.nytimes.com/...

China is using phone trackers to link people's digital lives to their physical movements. In one case, the documents revealed that the police from a county in Guangdong bought trackers in hopes of detecting a Uyghur-to-Chinese dictionary app on phones. https://www.nytimes.com/... https://twitter.com/...

Our video is based on 1000s of government bidding documents. There are many takeaways, I'll run through a few. The first, is just how meticulous police are in deciding what goes where. Camera placements are thought out meticulously. Police point precisely to locations and angles. https://twitter.com/...
Coming soon to a moderately less authoritarian state near you https://www.nytimes.com/...

DNA, iris scans and voice prints are being collected indiscriminately from people with no known connection to crime. In the city of Zhongshan, the police wanted devices to record audio in public from 300 to 600 feet around cameras and analyze voice prints. https://www.nytimes.com/... https://twitter.com/...
This new video from the Visual Investigations team @nytimes outlines the current capabilities and future ambitions of China's surveillance systems. https://www.nytimes.com/...

Cameras go in hotel and hospital lobbies. In Fujian a Sheraton gave police access to the cameras INSIDE its hotel. Likely many other international hotels do too. With China boasting half of the world's one billion cameras, data collection can be immense. https://www.nytimes.com/...
The Khronos Group forms the Metaverse Standards Forum with Nvidia, Meta, Epic Games, Unity, Microsoft, W3C, and others, seeking to improve interoperability
From Nvidia to IKEA: Here's who's joining the Metaverse Standards Forum
Khronos Group launches standards effort for an open and interoperable metaverse
New Standards Forum Meets In July To Plan Metaverse Interoperability
Tech giants opt into newly-formed Metaverse Standards Forum
Nvidia, Meta and others team up to form Metaverse Standards Forum
NVIDIA Joins Forum to Help Lay the Foundation of the Metaverse
Major XR Players Join New Metaverse Forum to Cooperate on Foundational Standards
Tech Giants Unite to Save Metaverse from Being the Wild West
Leading Standards Organizations and Companies Unite to Drive Open Metaverse Interoperability
Leading Standards Organizations and Companies Unite to Drive Open Metaverse Interoperability

Meta, Epic Games, NVIDIA, Microsoft, and many other tech giants are coming together to agree upon open standards for the metaverse, via the newly-launched Metaverse Standards Forum. Definitely one of the bigger announcements about the metaverse this year. https://metaverse-standards.org/ https://twitter.com/...
Kinda like the Climate Group but with delusion included https://twitter.com/...
The group includes representation from most of the big players in the emerging metaverse, including Nvidia, Meta, Epic Games, Unity, Microsoft, and the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C).

Interoperability — or lack thereof — is a key issue in the nascent #Metaverse industry. A new #OpenStandards discussion group called The Metaverse Standards Forum launches today to address this. I spoke to @neilt3d from @thekhronosgroup about the launch. https://thenewstack.io/...

@Meta, @Google, @nvidia, @Qualcomm and @Adobe are among the companies uniting behind ‘The Metaverse Standards Forum’, a consortium announced today (June 21) to prop up dialog on the underpinnings of the #metaverse. #web3 https://www.enterpriseai.news/ ...
In all my 'metaverse doesn't exist yet' talks, the lack of a standards forum has been a key proof point. We should welcome this. Smart. About time. https://twitter.com/...

These are the current holdouts, but it seems to me that Metaverse Standards Forum has most of the big names. I'm interested in whether Meta, Epic and Unity will now be more pro-web in their metaverse dev, going forward. https://twitter.com/...

The Metaverse Standards Forum has been formed to foster development of open standards to build the open metaverse. Founding members include 10 standard organizations and 25+ top tech companies. Come join us! https://metaverse-standards.org/ ... #metaverse #standards #opensource

To be fair, Apple hasn't said much of anything at all as best I know about the metaverse, but it is developing AR/VR/mixed reality devices. Roblox and Niantic certainly have reoriented a lot of their business around the metaverse, but both focus mainly on mobile right now.

Like the internet, the metaverse will not be built by one company alone - it must be open and interoperable, which will enable people to move seamlessly between virtual experiences regardless of who built them, or what device they use to access them
Some notable exclusions from the founding members list of the Metaverse Standards Forum: Apple, Niantic, and Roblox. https://twitter.com/...

I'm excited to share that Meta is joining the Metaverse Standards Forum that brings together leading standards organizations and companies to work together on interoperability standards needed to build the open metaverse: https://metaverse-standards.org/

We need to make sure that we are leaders in semiconductor chips. We can't just rely on others. We need to pass the Chips/USICA and get fabs here in US. It's for national security and for growing our economy, and leading in everything tech and science. https://www.wsj.com/...

But the point of US export controls is to confine China's chip industry to older “nodes”, restricting its ability to make the most advanced devices. Not to prevent sales growth. That has been successful. ⁦@ACGeoEcon⁩ ⁦@ACGlobalChina⁩ https://www.bloomberg.com/...

Chase Coleman is a case study of what is wrong with markets. Born on third base,he incinerates billions with his reckless strategy but personally retains big $$$. Shameful, truly shameful. Chase and ⁦@CathieDWood⁩ are made for each other. https://nymag.com/...

Some SMB owners complain Google is ending its G Suite legacy free edition after over a decade on August 1, arguing the company lured them with a free service
Google set to force G Suite freeloaders to cough up - and businesses are mad
End of the road for biz living off free G Suite legacy edition

Many millions of people have put huge parts of their digital lives on Google under the assumption that its services would stay free. (Or at least the kind of “free” where you're paying with data, not money.) But Google can always turn the screws. https://www.nytimes.com/...

Last week it was Brex, this week it's Google saying they're no longer interested in low (or in this case, zero) margin SMB customers. Expect more companies to effectively fire customers over the next few weeks as the cheap money party ends. https://www.nytimes.com/...

This new report on the demise of the “G Suite legacy free edition” greatly undermines Google's argument it's fighting for small business, an argument it's used against new privacy rules and antitrust legislation to limit harms of monopoly gatekeepers. https://www.nytimes.com/...

Rip you off? Not to praise Google, but no one's entitled to free services. 'When Mr. Sajanlal told Mr. Jiwani that Google would start to charge for each of their email addresses, Mr. Jiwani said: “Are you serious? They're going to start ripping us off?"' https://www.nytimes.com/...
“They're basically strong-arming us to switch to something paid after they got us hooked on this free service.” exactly https://twitter.com/...

Of course, the shittiest part of all this shitty stuff is that using G Suite actually cuts you off from a lot of Google product functionality. The Google Home assistant, Nest app and other stuff abruptly stops working with no explanation. So frustrating. https://www.nytimes.com/...

Google is charging some small businesses for email and other apps after more than a decade of free use as the company looks for ways to get more money out of its existing business. “It struck me as needlessly petty,” one user said. https://www.nytimes.com/...

Google alienated small business owners who were among its earliest email and app users, after demanding they pay for services they've gotten free for years. Some of the shops are recovering from the pandemic and felt Google was being “needlessly petty.” https://www.nytimes.com/...
Replacing ad revenue based on scanning “free” email correspondence? https://www.nytimes.com/...
Ah, more fallout from the G Suite Legacy wars. This will misadventure will one day have its own @HarvardHBS case. https://twitter.com/...
A sign that Google's growth is slowing. https://www.nytimes.com/...
This is a great point https://twitter.com/...
Analysis: despite claiming to reject gun ads, Google accepted ads from the 15 top US firearms sellers between March 9 and June 6, generating 120M+ impressions

Facebook needs to take gun sales on its platforms seriously, because companies like Facebook have a responsibility to help end gun violence. Add your name now if you agree: https://act.everytown.org/... @momsdemand

Ad retargeting is one reason why gun ads end up on sites that don't want them. Companies like Glock & Sig Sauer use Google ad tech to help target ads. If you visit https://glock.com/ and look at a gun, it logs your activity and can show you ads for that gun on other websites

Several mainstream publishers told us they don't accept gun ads. So how did this happen? Loopholes in Google's highly touted ban on gun ads, the use of Google's ad targeting tech by major gun sellers, and oversight failures by Google & its partners. Let's dive in.

Spokespeople for Heavy, The Denver Post, U.S. News & World Report, Publishers Clearing House & MacRumors said gun ads shouldn't have appeared on their sites. Playbuzz said it was launching an internal investigation after being contacted. https://twitter.com/...

Ads for pistols and rifles loaded on websites like The Denver Post, Playbuzz, Britannica, Merriam-Webster, and recipe sites. They were placed on Baby Games, amid brightly colored games for children, and on a parenting article about “How to Handle Teen Drama.” https://twitter.com/...

“The truth of it is Google makes money while looking the other way” “The tech giant has long boasted that it doesn't accept ads for firearms, but a @ProPublica analysis shows that #Google's ad systems served up more than 100 million ads from #gun makers.” https://www.propublica.org/...

“We don't allow gun ads,” said Google cofounder Sergey Brin in 2004, saying it was part of Google's “don't be evil” values. But 15 of the largest US firearms sellers used Google's systems to place ads that generated over 120M impressions between 3/9-6/6 https://www.propublica.org/...
He compared Google's approach to a mullet: “Google has corporate policies in the front and exchange-of-exchange internet chaos in the back.” https://twitter.com/...
